description_long:
Shipglass sells the separation of deploy from release. Teams merge code continuously, then release it deliberately — to 1 percent of users, to a cohort, to a region, to a plan — and when something breaks, the kill switch is instant: no revert, no hotfix train. That is the feature-flag pitch in general, and Shipglass is a straight-ahead, well-armed version of it.

The engineering claims are concrete. SDKs for 12 languages evaluate flags locally from a streamed ruleset, so evaluation adds well under 1 millisecond and keeps working offline; an edge network keeps rulesets within 50 milliseconds of users worldwide. Targeting combines user attributes, cohorts, and percentage rollouts. Two housekeeping features suggest the team has lived with flags at scale: every change is audited, and stale flags are surfaced for cleanup automatically — flag debt being the tax every adopter eventually pays.

Experimentation is folded in rather than sold separately: any rollout can become an A/B test by attaching a metric, with lift computed with confidence intervals. For teams that would otherwise run a flag system and an experimentation stack side by side, that consolidation is a quiet part of the value.

The intended user is a product engineering team that ships daily, and the site names first uses that map to real pain: risky migrations, tiered entitlements, and gradual mobile rollouts where app-store review makes rollback expensive. Pricing is a standard ladder — free up to 5 seats, Pro at $12 per seat per month, Enterprise with SAML, custom roles, and a dedicated environment.

Founded in Denver in 2021, the 27-person team previously built release tooling at large consumer-software companies, and the site is refreshingly direct about the competitive landscape: the homegrown alternative — a config table and a prayer — is its favorite competitor. That is the honest shape of this market; the product's case is that audit trails, staleness detection, and sub-millisecond evaluation are what the config table never grows.
